summary:
During the late [[event:55|Shang Dynasty]], rulers and diviners inscribed questions on the flat bones of cattle and the shells of turtles to communicate with royal ancestors and divine spirits. These oracle bones record the earliest substantial corpus of Chinese writing, with inscriptions dating primarily from approximately 1200 to 1050 BC at the capital Yin, modern Anyang in Henan Province. After a heated bone cracked, diviners interpreted the pattern of fissures as an ancestral response, then recorded the question, the crack reading, and sometimes the subsequent outcome.

The [[event:1840|oracle bone script]] is recognizably ancestral to modern Chinese characters, with logographic signs representing objects, concepts, and phonetic values. Tens of thousands of oracle bones have been recovered since their systematic excavation began in the late nineteenth century. Topics inscribed include royal hunts, military campaigns, weather forecasts, harvest prospects, illnesses of the king, and ritual schedules, providing an extraordinary window into Shang court life and administrative concerns.

The oracle bone archive effectively ends with the Zhou conquest in 1046 BC, as the succeeding [[event:63|Zhou Dynasty]] shifted to bronze vessel inscriptions as the primary medium of commemorative and administrative writing. The oracle bone script remained unknown to modern scholars until the 1890s, when pharmacists in Beijing recognized the inscribed bones being sold as dragon bones for medicinal use, prompting the first serious scholarly investigations.
